title: What a $20K PayPal supplier payment actually costs

scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
scripts.original_vo: Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you're getting destroyed and you don't even see it. Here's the math. You send 20K to your supplier. The receipt says 20K. The actual cost? Way higher. PayPal hits you with a 4.4% cross-border fee. That's 880 dollars gone. Then they take another 3.5% on the exchange rate. That's another 700 dollars. So your visible cost just hit 1,580. But it gets worse. Stop. Your supplier knows PayPal charges them on the receiving side too. So they pad your invoice 4 to 5%. That's another 800 dollars baked into the PI. Total damage on a 20K order? 2,380 dollars. Eleven percent gone. WorldFirst was built for this. Specialist rate. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same supplier. Hundreds in fees, not thousands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Let us walk you through what really happens when you pay a Chinese supplier through PayPal. Say you run a small business. You buy products from a factory in China. You owe them twenty thousand dollars for your next order. So you open PayPal and send the money. The screen says twenty thousand. Done, right? Not really. Here is what is going on behind that screen. First, PayPal takes a four point four percent fee for sending money across borders. That is eight hundred and eighty dollars gone. Then PayPal also changes your dollars into Chinese money, and they add three and a half percent on top of the real exchange rate. That is another seven hundred dollars. So far you have lost one thousand five hundred and eighty dollars and the receipt does not show most of it. Now here is the part most people miss. Your supplier in China also pays a fee when they get PayPal money. So to protect themselves, they quietly add four or five percent onto your invoice. That is another eight hundred dollars hidden inside the price they quoted you. Add it all up. Your twenty thousand dollar order actually cost you about twenty two thousand three hundred and eighty dollars. That is eleven percent of your money gone. We built WorldFirst for this exact problem. We give you a specialist exchange rate, and we send Chinese money straight to Chinese bank accounts. Same supplier, same order, but the fees drop from thousands to hundreds. Just search WorldFirst.

Why? 4.4% cross-border fee, that's 880 dollars. 3.5% FX margin, that's another 700. Plus a 4 to 5% supplier markup because they're absorbing PayPal's receiving fee. Add it up: 2,380 dollars per order. Now run it for a year. Twelve orders. That's 28,560 dollars handed to a payments app and a padded invoice line. Three years? Over 85,000 dollars. That's a hire. scripts.subtitle: Mechanism-first reveal
scripts.original_vo: Here's the problem most business owners don't realize when they use PayPal for Chinese suppliers. The fee is not the fee. You see PayPal charged 4.4%, that's 880 dollars on a 20K order. You think that's the cost. Wrong. The exchange rate margin is the bigger bite. PayPal marks up USD to CNY by roughly 3.5%, hidden inside the rate. On 20K, that's another 700 dollars. Visible total: 1,580. Then the third layer. Your Chinese supplier knows PayPal charges them too. So they pad the PI 4 to 5%, around 800 dollars on a 20K quote. Three layers, one invoice. Real cost? 2,380 on a 20K order. PayPal is built for retail. Not 20K weekly supplier payments. Stop. WorldFirst is built for this exact use case. Specialist FX. Direct CNY rails. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Let us share something most people are not telling you about PayPal supplier payments. The receipt is not the real cost. Picture this. You send twenty thousand dollars to your factory in China. PayPal confirms twenty thousand dollars. Job done, right? Not quite. Let us break down what really happened. First, PayPal took four point four percent as a cross border fee. That is eight hundred and eighty dollars. You saw that one on the receipt. Then PayPal added three and a half percent on top of the exchange rate when they changed your dollars into Chinese money. That is another seven hundred dollars. You did not see this one because it was hidden inside the rate. Then your supplier added four or five percent to the invoice because they get charged a fee on their side when they receive PayPal money. That is another eight hundred dollars hiding in your price. Add it up and the real damage on a twenty thousand dollar order is two thousand three hundred and eighty dollars. That is almost twelve percent gone. PayPal is great for everyday shopping. It is just the wrong tool for twenty thousand dollar supplier orders. We built WorldFirst differently. Specialist exchange rates. Chinese money sent straight to Chinese banks. The rate you see is the rate you get. That is the real difference. Just search WorldFirst.

That's $800 hiding in your PI. Total damage on a $20K order? $2,380. Almost 12 percent. PayPal is brilliant for retail. It's the wrong tool for $20K supplier orders. WorldFirst is built differently. scripts.original_vo: Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China with PayPal. You think PayPal is convenient. It is. It's also expensive. You send $20,000 to your supplier. PayPal takes $880 on the cross-border fee. Then $700 more on the FX margin nobody warned you about. Then your supplier hands you a PI with another 4 to 5% padded in because PayPal charges them too. By the time the goods ship, that $20K order cost you $22,380. Plus a 21-day fund hold on the supplier side that they price in next time. I see this every single week. SMEs paying retail prices for B2B volume because the app is familiar. Stop. WorldFirst. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Full $20K lands. Supplier doesn't pad. You sleep. It is also expensive. Picture this. You send twenty thousand dollars to your supplier. PayPal takes eight hundred and eighty dollars as a fee for sending money to another country. Then PayPal adds another seven hundred dollars on top of the exchange rate when they turn your dollars into Chinese money, and nobody warned you about this one. PH1.analysis: Core fee math (4.4% + 3.5% FX + 4-5% supplier markup) is defensible and grounded in source findings, but the total '$2,380 on $20K = 11%' is stacked aggressively and assumes the worst-case supplier markup applies universally. 'Eleven percent gone' is a sharp framing that a skeptic with lower historical fee experience will challenge. The 'WorldFirst hundreds in fees, not thousands' line is broadly accurate per source data but lacks a specific number, which weakens the contrast on follow-up.

skeptic_comments.text: My supplier never pad my PI for PayPal lah. Where this 4-5% from?
PayPal fee for me is 3.9% not 4.4%. Numbers feel off.
Eleven percent? That's only if everything stacks worst case. Not always.
Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before. They got license in MY?
Show me real rate comparison side by side la, not just claim.

PH2.analysis: The compounding extrapolation (one order a month for three years = $85K) is the highest-risk claim. It assumes constant order size, constant fee structure, constant supplier markup, and no negotiation across 36 months. 'Your kid's school fees' is emotive layered onto an unverifiable extrapolation. 'PayPal pricing built for retail, not 20K supplier payments' is defensible as a category statement but the math chain is shakier than the individual fee figures.

skeptic_comments.text: 85K over 3 years? You assume I send 20K every month never stop. Cooked.
My order size varies. Sometimes 5K sometimes 30K. This math doesn't reflect reality.
Kid's school fees? Bro really pulling on heartstrings now.
PayPal got business rates that lower right? You picking retail tier to make it sound bad.
Specialist FX rates means what exactly? Show me the actual WorldFirst rate.

PH3.analysis: 'Your PayPal receipt only tells part of the story' is softened from the original 'lying' and now stays factually accurate because PayPal's receipts do disclose fees in the breakdown, just not the FX margin or the supplier-side markup. The mechanism reveal is solid. Weakest spot is still the 4-5% supplier markup as universal, which depends entirely on the supplier's policy. 'WorldFirst publishes the rate' is true for the WF business product but not a specific number, so on follow-up a skeptic will want to see it.

skeptic_comments.text: Receipt also show fee la. PayPal not hiding it.
Supplier markup for PayPal not universal. Mine never charge me extra.
WorldFirst publishes the rate - what rate today? Need to see number to compare.
FX margin 3.5%? Sometimes PayPal market rate not so bad you know.
Where can I find this WorldFirst? Got app or website? You assume I know.

PH4.analysis: Mechanism-first reveal is the strongest of the five because it teaches the actual three-layer structure rather than just rolling out a total number. Numbers (4.4% / 3.5% / 4-5%) align with source findings. Risk is still the supplier markup being framed as default behaviour. 'Built for retail, not 20K weekly supplier payments' is fair as a category framing because PayPal's published pricing tiers do confirm this. 'WorldFirst specialist FX, direct CNY rails' is accurate to source but vague on actual rate vs PayPal headline.

skeptic_comments.text: Three layers ok I follow. But 4-5% supplier pad - need proof. Not all do.
PayPal got business merchant tier with 2.99%. You only show retail rate.
Direct CNY rails sound nice. What's the actual margin WorldFirst takes?
Built for retail. Then explain why so many SMEs still use it for B2B.
I'd switch but I don't know if WorldFirst supports my country MY.

PH5.analysis: The three-layer problem stack is structurally similar to PH4 but framed as 'you only saw one of them' which is sharper. The fee figures are solid. 'Almost 12 percent' on a $20K order is the bold number to defend and it does sit at the upper end of the range. The 'WorldFirst is worth a look' brand-search CTA is correctly softened. Weakest spot is still treating supplier markup as inevitable rather than situational. Risk also includes 'reputation with that supplier' which doesn't appear in this version but the 12% framing alone may push skeptics to push back on math composition.

skeptic_comments.text: 12% leak? My PayPal cost feels more like 5-6% total. You're stacking worst case.
If supplier really pads 4-5% why my Foshan guy never do? You're generalising.
Three things I didn't see? Bro the fee is right there on the receipt, FX too sometimes.
WorldFirst is worth a look ok but what license they got in SEA?
Specialist FX, direct CNY rails - need to see the actual product, not just claim.

PG1.analysis: Numbers align with source data on the visible PayPal side. The supplier markup at 4-5% is the soft spot, as in the Hormozi set, because it relies on the source claim that 'some suppliers add 4-5%' generalised to a default. 'Retail app pricing wholesale volume' is the strongest category-framing line and survives challenge well because PayPal's own tier structure confirms it. 'Specialist FX, direct CNY to Chinese banks' is accurate to source on WF.

skeptic_comments.text: $2,380 on $20K? My last PayPal supplier payment cost much less.
4-5% PI padding is hearsay. My supplier never tell me this is for PayPal.
Retail app pricing wholesale volume - ok fair point but need real alternative comparison.
WorldFirst sounds like another PayPal that just doesn't have brand yet.
Hundreds not thousands - show me a real screenshot lah, not just talk.

PG2.analysis: Compounding to $85K over three years is the highest-risk frame in the Gary Vee set, same critique as PH2. Plus 'most overpriced thing in your supply chain' is a category-wide claim that a skeptic with Wise or Payoneer experience will dispute because bank wires and crypto rails can both be worse. The line 'hidden in the rate' is correct but applied to PayPal which does have FX disclosure on the conversion screen, even if buried.

skeptic_comments.text: 85K over 3 years assume I pay every month never miss. Not real.
Most overpriced? Got crypto fees worse, got banks worse. You exaggerating.
PayPal does show the FX rate before I confirm. Not exactly hidden.
Supplier pads PI 4-5% for PayPal - is this every supplier? Mine don't.
Where does the money go back into business? You skip the part where I have to migrate.

PG3.analysis: Right-tool-for-the-job analogy is clean and survives challenge well. Math layers (880 + 700 + 800 = 2,380) are arithmetic-accurate. The weakest spot is the assumption that switching to WorldFirst means supplier won't pad the PI, which is true if WF settles in CNY directly but a skeptic may not know that mechanism. 'Use the right tool for the job, your margin depends on it' is a brand-safe close.

skeptic_comments.text: Credit card to buy house analogy - too dramatic. PayPal works fine for most.
If WF pays CNY direct, supplier still need to verify and clear. Same delay or not?
Supplier don't pad PI for me. You assume my guy is greedy.
Specialist FX what rate ah? Need the actual number to decide.
Margin depends on it - ok but switching is also a cost. Time, paperwork, KYC.

PG4.analysis: Three-layer truth structure is the same mechanism as PH4 but in Gary Vee voice. Strongest line is 'PayPal is brilliant for retail. It's the wrong tool for $20K supplier orders' which is defensible category framing. Weakest is still the supplier markup as universal. 'Rate you see is rate you get' is a soft claim that's directionally true for WF business but not exactly literal at the receipt level because there's still a small spread.

skeptic_comments.text: Rate you see is rate you get - so WorldFirst takes zero margin? Sure or not?
Brilliant for retail wrong tool for B2B - actually this part fair.
Almost 12% only if supplier really adds 4-5%. Not standard for everyone.
WorldFirst direct CNY to Chinese banks - they have license for SAFE compliance?
I can audit PayPal cost. How do I audit WorldFirst cost before I switch?

PG5.analysis: Adds the 21-day fund hold detail from source findings which strengthens credibility on the supplier-side cost. 'Plus a 21-day fund hold on the supplier side that they price in next time' is exactly the kind of mechanism-grounded detail that survives a skeptic's poke. Risk remains the universal supplier markup framing. 'Supplier doesn't pad' on the WF side is a soft claim because some suppliers still pad regardless of rail, just for cushion.

skeptic_comments.text: 21-day fund hold true for PayPal but mostly for new sellers right? Established suppliers maybe not.
Supplier doesn't pad with WorldFirst - how you know? Maybe they pad regardless.
$22,380 on $20K - this assume all worst case. My orders never hit this much.
Convenient is expensive ok fair. But switching also has friction cost.
Specialist FX, direct CNY - I keep hearing this. Just show me the rate today.
